g.data-narration: Welcome to Heterogeneous Computing - the foundation of modern GPU programming using CUDA.
Heterogeneous computing combines CPUs and GPUs with separate memory spaces to maximize performance through specialization.
g.text: Systems using multiple processor types (CPU + GPU) with separate memory spaces.
CPU handles control-intensive sequential code; GPU handles data-parallel computation.
Programmer explicitly manages data movement between host (CPU) and device (GPU) memory.

g.data-narration: In CUDA, the Host is the CPU with system RAM, while the Device is the GPU with dedicated DRAM memory.
A typical CUDA program follows seven steps: initialize, allocate memory, transfer data, launch kernel, execute, retrieve results, and cleanup.
The cudaMalloc function allocates memory on the GPU device, while cudaFree releases it when no longer needed.
The cudaMemcpy function transfers data between host and device, specifying the direction: H2D, D2H, D2D, or H2H.
Synchronous transfers block the CPU until complete. Asynchronous transfers using streams allow overlapping computation and data movement.
Pinned (page-locked) memory cannot be swapped to disk, enabling higher PCIe bandwidth and asynchronous transfers via DMA.
g.text: ⚠ Warning: Don't allocate too much - reduces OS paging memory

g.data-narration: Unified Memory creates a single address space accessible from both CPU and GPU, with automatic data migration on page faults.
Use cudaMemPrefetchAsync to move data before it's needed, and cudaMemAdvise to hint access patterns to the runtime.
g.text: Hint: read mostly, preferred location, etc.

g.data-narration: PCIe bandwidth is the bottleneck in heterogeneous systems - at best 64 GB/s (Gen5) vs 2 TB/s GPU memory bandwidth.
Strategies to minimize PCIe overhead: keep data on GPU, use streams for overlap, batch transfers, and use pinned memory.
NVLink provides high-bandwidth direct GPU-to-GPU interconnect. GPUDirect enables direct data paths between GPU and storage/network.
Best practices: profile first, maximize compute-to-transfer ratio, use streams, and choose the right memory model for your needs.
Heterogeneous computing is the foundation of GPU programming. Master memory management and data transfer to write high-performance CUDA applications.
g.text: • Pinned memory enables async transfers and higher bandwidth
• Unified Memory simplifies but has page-fault overhead
• PCIe is bottleneck - minimize transfers, use streams

g.data-narration: Now you understand the heterogeneous computing model. Apply this knowledge to write efficient CUDA programs!
